title: Go Under the Hood
subtitle: Deep Dive Series
author: Mert Unsal
description:
Runtime-level explanations of Go's core systems —
what the compiler and runtime actually do with your code.
Slices · Maps · Strings · Pointers · Closures · Interfaces · Generics · Error Handling
Goroutines · Channels · GC · Memory · Testing · Debugging · Production Patterns
by Mert Unsal
subject: Runtime-level explanations of Go's core systems
keywords: Go, Golang, internals, runtime, compiler, concurrency, goroutines, channels, GC, memory, production

parts.name: Part I — Data Structures In Depth

howto_text:
## Who This Is For

This book is for **senior engineers** transitioning to or deepening mastery in Go. It assumes you already know programming fundamentals and focuses on what makes Go different at the runtime and compiler level.

## Structure

The book is organized in **9 parts**, each building on the previous:

- **Parts I–II** — Data structures & language mechanics: how Go lays out memory
- **Part III** — Type system: interfaces, boxing, and the `any` type
- **Part IV** — Error handling: wrapping chains and concurrent patterns
- **Part V** — Concurrency: the GMP scheduler, stacks, and channel internals
- **Part VI** — Runtime: GC, memory allocator, escape analysis, sorting
- **Part VII** — Testing & debugging: test internals, pprof, trace, dlv
- **Part VIII** — Design & architecture: philosophy, middleware, production patterns
- **Part IX** — Cross-cutting: context as an interface design masterclass

## Navigation

- **Table of Contents** — Click any chapter title to jump directly to it
- **Bookmark Panel** — Open your PDF reader's sidebar for the full Part → Chapter hierarchy
- **Running Headers** — The current chapter name appears at the top of every page
- **Page Numbers** — Centered at the bottom, matching your reader's page indicator

## Depth Layers

Every chapter follows a consistent five-layer depth pattern:

| Layer | What It Covers |
|-------|----------------|
| **1: WHAT** | The API and syntax (briefly — you can read docs) |
| **2: HOW** | What the compiler and runtime do under the hood |
| **3: WHY** | The design decision and tradeoffs behind it |
| **4: BREAKS** | Edge cases, failure modes, production gotchas |
| **5: AT SCALE** | Behavior at 10k goroutines, 100k connections |

## Code & Diagrams

- Code blocks show Go source, compiler output, and assembly
- ASCII diagrams illustrate memory layouts and runtime structures
- Compiler flags like `go build -gcflags='-m'` are referenced throughout

## Companion Repository

This book accompanies the **go-interview-prep** repository with runnable exercises, tests, and solutions for hands-on practice.

## Suggested Reading Order

- **New to Go?** Start with Part I (Data Structures) and Part II (Language Mechanics)
- **Know Go basics?** Jump to Part V (Concurrency) — where Go truly differs
- **Production focus?** Parts VII–VIII cover debugging and enterprise patterns
author_page:
### Mert Unsal

*Senior Software Engineer*

---

I am a senior software engineer with over **7 years of experience** building production systems across multiple languages and tech stacks. When I decided to deepen my mastery of Go, I found that most resources fell into two categories: beginner tutorials that explain what a goroutine *is*, or API references that list function signatures. Neither answered the question I kept asking: **what is the runtime actually doing with my code?**

This book was born from that curiosity.

### Why This Book Exists

I believe that if you want to work with any programming language professionally, you must understand its **essential principles and mechanics** - not just its syntax. You need to know *why* a slice is a three-word header, *how* the scheduler decides which goroutine runs next, and *what* happens in memory when you close a channel.

Every page in this book reflects that philosophy:

- **No boilerplate explanations.** If every developer already knows it, it is not here.
- **No trivial information.** Only details that are genuinely worth mentioning.
- **Personal perspective.** This is not a textbook - it is a working engineer's deep dive into Go's internals, written while building real systems.

### What You Will Find

Each chapter goes under the hood: runtime source code references, compiler flag outputs, memory layout diagrams, escape analysis traces, and production gotchas I encountered firsthand. The goal is to build the mental model that lets you **predict** what Go will do before you run the code.

### Open Source

This book and its companion repository are publicly available. Contributions, corrections, and discussions are welcome:

[github.com/mert-unsal/go-learning-guide](https://github.com/mert-unsal/go-learning-guide)
references_page:
This book draws on the following primary sources. All runtime source code references point to the Go standard library, which is open source and readable at [go.googlesource.com/go](https://go.googlesource.com/go).

### Go Runtime Source Code

The following files from the Go runtime are referenced throughout:

- **runtime/slice.go** - Slice creation, growslice(), capacity growth formulas (Chapter 01)
- **runtime/map.go** - Map implementation: hmap, bmap, bucket arrays, evacuation (Chapter 02)
- **runtime/string.go** - String header, immutability enforcement, conversions (Chapter 03)
- **runtime/proc.go** - GMP scheduler: schedule(), findRunnable(), work stealing (Chapter 10)
- **runtime/stack.go** - Contiguous stacks, newstack(), stack growth/shrinking (Chapter 11)
- **runtime/chan.go** - Channel internals: hchan, send(), recv(), selectgo() (Chapter 12)
- **runtime/mgc.go** - Garbage collector: tri-color marking, write barriers, pacing (Chapter 13)
- **runtime/malloc.go** - Memory allocator: mcache, mcentral, mheap, size classes (Chapter 13)
- **runtime/iface.go** - Interface dispatch: iface, eface, itab caching (Chapter 06)
- **runtime/select.go** - Select statement: case shuffling, lock ordering (Chapter 12)
- **runtime/panic.go** - Panic/recover mechanics, defer implementation (Chapter 05)

### Go Language Specification

- [The Go Programming Language Specification](https://go.dev/ref/spec) - The authoritative reference for Go semantics
- [Effective Go](https://go.dev/doc/effective_go) - Idiomatic Go patterns and conventions

### Official Go Blog and Design Documents

- [Go Blog](https://go.dev/blog/) - Official announcements and deep-dive articles
- [Go Slices: Usage and Internals](https://go.dev/blog/slices-intro) - Andrew Gerrand
- [Go Maps in Action](https://go.dev/blog/maps) - Andrew Gerrand
- [Strings, bytes, runes and characters in Go](https://go.dev/blog/strings) - Rob Pike
- [Share Memory By Communicating](https://go.dev/blog/codelab-share) - Andrew Gerrand
- [Go Concurrency Patterns](https://go.dev/blog/pipelines) - Sameer Ajmani
- [Introducing the Go Race Detector](https://go.dev/blog/race-detector) - Dmitry Vyukov
- [Profiling Go Programs](https://go.dev/blog/pprof) - Russ Cox
- [Getting to Go: The Journey of Go's Garbage Collector](https://go.dev/blog/ismmkeynote) - Rick Hudson
- [A Guide to the Go Garbage Collector](https://tip.golang.org/doc/gc-guide) - Official GC tuning guide

### Go Proposals and Design Docs

- [Contiguous Stacks Design](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1wAaf1rYoM4nQswJaxbbPcKVAMELnm9CgoAclQkXpE0s) - Chapter 11
- [Generics: GC Shape Stenciling](https://github.com/golang/proposal/blob/master/design/generics-implementation-dictionaries-go1.18.md) - Chapter 07
- [Loop Variable Semantics (Go 1.22)](https://go.dev/blog/loopvar-preview) - Chapter 05
- [Structured Logging with slog](https://go.dev/blog/slog) - Chapter 18

### Talks and Presentations

- **Go Proverbs** - Rob Pike, GopherFest 2015 - The philosophical foundation of Go
- **Concurrency Is Not Parallelism** - Rob Pike, Waza 2013 - CSP model and goroutine design
- **The Scheduler Saga** - Kavya Joshi, GopherCon 2018 - Deep dive into GMP scheduling
- **Understanding Channels** - Kavya Joshi, GopherCon 2017 - Channel internals with runtime source
- **Garbage Collection in Go** - Rick Hudson, GopherCon 2018 - GC evolution and design

### Tools Referenced

- **go tool pprof** - CPU, memory, goroutine, block, and mutex profiling (Chapter 15)
- **go tool trace** - Execution tracer with visual timeline (Chapter 15)
- **dlv (Delve)** - Go-aware debugger: [github.com/go-delve/delve](https://github.com/go-delve/delve) (Chapter 15)
- **go build -gcflags='-m'** - Escape analysis output (used throughout)
- **go build -gcflags='-S'** - Assembly output for inspecting generated code
- **go test -race** - Data race detector (Chapter 14)
- **GODEBUG environment variable** - Runtime debugging flags: gctrace=1, schedtrace=1000 (Chapter 15)
- **golangci-lint** - Meta-linter: [github.com/golangci/golangci-lint](https://github.com/golangci/golangci-lint)
- **fieldalignment** - Struct field ordering optimizer from golang.org/x/tools

### Books and External Resources

- *The Go Programming Language* - Alan Donovan and Brian Kernighan (Addison-Wesley, 2015)
- *Concurrency in Go* - Katherine Cox-Buday (O'Reilly, 2017)
- *100 Go Mistakes and How to Avoid Them* - Teiva Harsanyi (Manning, 2022)
- [Go by Example](https://gobyexample.com/) - Annotated example programs
- [Go Wiki](https://go.dev/wiki/) - Community-maintained knowledge base

### Companion Repository

The source code, exercises, and all markdown files for this book are available at:

**[github.com/mert-unsal/go-learning-guide](https://github.com/mert-unsal/go-learning-guide)**

Contributions, corrections, and discussions are welcome.
